title: Graph theoretic derivation of mutual linearity for transient probabilities and hitting time distributions in Markov networks
authors: Julian B. Voits, Ulrich S. Schwarz
abstract: For irreducible, time-homogeneous Markov networks, mutual linearity has recently been established for both occupation probabilities and network currents in the stationary regime as well as in the non-stationary regime in Laplace space. The derivation of this property for the stationary distribution utilized the Markov-chain tree theorem, which also allows for an explicit combinatorial expression of the response ratios under variation of a single transition rate. The extension of this result was proven at the trajectory level by employing the Doob-Meyer decomposition. By employing the all-minors matrix-tree theorem, we show that this property also follows from a graph theoretic formulation and derive explicit combinatorial expressions for the non-stationary response ratios. The stationary result follows as the long-time limit and we also show that the small-time asymptotics are entirely determined by minimal path distances in the underlying graph. Finally we use the graph theoretic approach to prove that mutual linearity also extends to hitting time densities.
summary: This paper provides a new graph-theoretic proof for the mutual linearity of transient probabilities and hitting time distributions in Markov networks. By using the all-minorable matrix-tree theorem, the authors derive explicit combinatorial formulas for how these distributions respond to changes in transition rates. The results bridge the gap between stationary and non-stationary dynamics and show that short-time responses are determined by graph geometry.
main_problem: The paper seeks to establish a graph-theoretic derivation for the 'mutual linearity' property in Markov networks, extending it from the stationary regime to the non-stationary (transient) regime in Laplace space.
main_result: The authors prove that mutual linearity applies to both transient occupation probabilities and hitting time distributions, providing explicit combinatorial expressions for non-stationary response ratios and susceptibilities.
method: The study employs the all-minors matrix-tree theorem, Laplace transforms, and combinatorial tools like spanning forests and trees on augmented graphs to derive the results.
model_or_system: The system consists of irreducible, time-homogeneous Markov networks defined by a continuous-time stochastic process on a directed, edge-weighted graph where dynamics are governed by the Kolmogorov forward equation.
key_observables: Occupation probabilities, network currents, response ratios, susceptibility, and hitting time distributions.
important_parameters: Transition rates (k_ij), Laplace variable (s), stationary regime (long-time limit), and short-time asymptotics (governed by minimal path distances).
main_assumptions: The Markov networks are assumed to be irreducible and time-homogeneous.
figures_summary: Figure 1 visualizes graph theoretic concepts like directed graphs and spanning forests; Figure 5 illustrates an auxiliary map for edge exchange; Figure 6 shows short-time scaling of response ratios in an example network.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of mutual linearity, develops a graph-theoretic framework using the all-minors matrix-tree theorem, extends the derivation to the non-stationary regime in Laplace space, proves the extension to hitting time densities, and analyzes short-time and long-time limits.
why_interesting: This work provides fundamental mathematical constraints on the dynamics of stochastic networks, which is highly relevant for understanding the relaxation and response of open quantum systems and biochemical networks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Illustration of relevant graph theoretic concepts. (a) An example of a directed graph G with N = 7 vertices. (b) A path in G from vertex 1 to vertex 7. (c) A spanning forest of G with roots 1 and 6. (d) A spanning tree of G with root 5.

Figure 2. Illustration of the construction that allows to express the Laplace transform of the occupation probabilities in graph theoretic terms. (a) An example for a Markov network on four states represented as a weighted and directed graph G with the states as vertices, the transitions as directed edges with the transition rates as edge weights. (b) The augmented graph G∗is constructed by including an additional vertex ∗and edges with rate s leading from every other vertex to ∗. (c) A spanning tree of the augmented graph with root in ∗. (d) A spanning forest containing two trees with roots in 3 and ∗.

Figure 3. Illustration of the mutual linearity for an example network. (a) Network with four vertices, where the rate k13 is varied. (b) Affine relation (mutual linearity) between the Laplace transforms of the occupation probabilities L{p}(s)1 and L{p}(s)2 for different values of s. The parameters are k12 = 1, k21 = 0.4, k23 = 1.2, k32 = 0.5, k34 = 0.9, k43 = 0.6, k41 = 1.1, k14 = 0.3, k31 = 0.2 and k13 ∈[0, 5]. The solution is obtained from a numerical inversion of Eq. (16). The transparent lines show the analytical solution for the slope, L{χ(1,3) 2,1 }(s) = k32−k12 s+k21+k23 , obtained from the graph theoretic expression, Eq. (31).
